reasoning: The central bank is currently in an interest rate easing/stabilization cycle, with the primary credit rate holding at 3.75% since early 2026. The next FOMC meeting is set for March 17-18, 2026, falling squarely between the forecast date (March 15) and resolution date (March 22). Current CME FedWatch data shows that markets overwhelmingly expect the Fed to hold rates steady (88-92% probability), with most of the remaining probability weighted toward a cut (as two Fed governors dissented in January in favor of a 25 bps cut). A rate hike is extremely unlikely and effectively unpriced by the market. Therefore, the likelihood of an increase in the primary credit rate over this 7-day window is negligibly small. While the FRED statistical tool provided a combined P(increase) of 0.46, this naive estimate is heavily distorted by comparing current rates with the higher rates of early 2025 (4.50%), and mistakenly interpreting this as a "seasonal" trend, failing to account for the actual monetary policy easing cycle.
